0x401f75 MOV	$0x2,%ECX

/home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90: 604 - 623
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


604: !$omp parallel do default(shared) private(i1,i2,i3,u1,u2) collapse(2)


605:       do i3=2,n3-1


606:          do i2=2,n2-1


607:             do i1=1,n1


608:                u1(i1) = u(i1,i2-1,i3) + u(i1,i2+1,i3)  &


609:      &                + u(i1,i2,i3-1) + u(i1,i2,i3+1)


610:                u2(i1) = u(i1,i2-1,i3-1) + u(i1,i2+1,i3-1)  &


611:      &                + u(i1,i2-1,i3+1) + u(i1,i2+1,i3+1)


612:             enddo


613:             do i1=2,n1-1


[...]


623:      &                     - a(3) * ( u2(i1-1) + u2(i1+1) )

Functionresid_._omp_fn.0

          
Source file and linesmg.f90:604-623

        
Modulemg_omp_f90_gfortran

        

The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:604-613,623.

Analyzed code is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:604,607-613,623.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 10066 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=10066
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths

Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.

This loop has 10066 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.33 to 72.00 cycles (1.41x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 37% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.33 to 76.71 cycles (1.32x speedup).

Details


41% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 51% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 36% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 65% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 5% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 70 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.00x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • ADD: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623

Vector unaligned load/store instructions


Detected 9 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.


Details


  • VMOVUPD: 9 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:609

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623


Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
  2. Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)




Type of elements and instruction set


41 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
13 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
13 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).
39 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).


Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop is composed of 501 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 396: addition or subtraction (70 inside FMA instructions)
  • 105: multiply (70 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 4409 bytes (551 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 888 bytes (111 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.09 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.


  


General properties


nb instructions562
nb uops608
loop length2990
used x86 registers15
used mmx registers0
used xmm registers24
used ymm registers7
used zmm registers18

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 101.33 cycles. At this rate:

  • 45% of peak load performance is reached (43.51 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 13% of peak store performance is reached (8.76 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

Functionresid_._omp_fn.0

          
Source file and linesmg.f90:604-623

        
Modulemg_omp_f90_gfortran

        

The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:604-613,623.

Analyzed code is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:604,607-613,623.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 10066 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=10066
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths

Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.

This loop has 10066 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.33 to 72.00 cycles (1.41x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 37% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.33 to 76.71 cycles (1.32x speedup).

Details


41% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 51% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 36% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 65% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 5% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 70 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.00x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).


  


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • ADD: 2 occurrences►

Vector unaligned load/store instructions


Detected 9 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.


Details


  • VMOVUPD: 9 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:609

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:623


Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
  2. Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)




Type of elements and instruction set


41 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
13 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
13 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).
39 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).


Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop


The binary loop is composed of 501 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 396: addition or subtraction (70 inside FMA instructions)
  • 105: multiply (70 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 4409 bytes (551 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 888 bytes (111 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.09 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.


  


General properties


nb instructions562
nb uops608

Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 101.33 cycles. At this rate:

Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).
